title: Symplectic geometry of hyperbolic cylinders and their homoclinic intersections
speaker: Jean-Pierre Marco
affiliation: Pierre and Marie Curie University - Paris 6
abstract: Abstract: We first examine the existence, uniqueness, regularity, twist and symplectic properties of compact invariant cylinders with boundary, located near simple or double resonances in perturbations of action-angle systems on the annulus $A^3$. We then prove they satisfy sufficient compatibility conditions on their dynamics and their homoclinic intersections, in order to prove the existence of drifting orbits along them, shadowing pseudo-orbits of inner-homoclinic polysystems.

proof_stages.label: Double-resonance normal form and target cylinder
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up a near-integrable Hamiltonian on the cotangent bundle of the three-torus near a double resonance, passes to a simplified normal form with a classical slow subsystem, identifies a compact hyperbolic annulus of periodic orbits, and states the goal of producing a normally hyperbolic invariant cylinder diffeomorphic to T^2 times an interval.

proof_stages.label: Graph theorem for normal hyperbolicity with boundary models
proof_stages.summary: After recalling standard and symplectic formulations of normal hyperbolicity, the speaker states a noncompact product-coordinate persistence theorem giving invariant, stable, and unstable graphs with regularity and quotient properties, and uses it to define normally hyperbolic manifolds with boundary in local coordinates.

proof_stages.label: Symplectic consequences for invariant manifolds
proof_stages.summary: In the Hamiltonian setting the persistent central manifold is shown to be symplectic, its stable and unstable manifolds coisotropic, and their characteristic foliations exactly the stable and unstable leaf foliations, yielding improved transverse regularity.

proof_stages.label: Straightening theorem and geometric normal form for the initial annulus
proof_stages.summary: A symplectic straightening theorem extends a symplectic annulus embedding to neighborhood coordinates that straighten the stable and unstable manifolds and leaves, and applying this to the classical annulus gives Liouville-Arnold coordinates and the Taylor normal form C=c0(rho)+lambda(phi,rho)us+O3(u,s).

proof_stages.label: Persistence of a rescaled truncated annulus
proof_stages.summary: For the double-resonance normal form without the smallest final remainder, the fast variables are rescaled on the universal cover and the hyperbolic variables are scaled so that the graph theorem yields a normally hyperbolic symplectic four-dimensional annulus A_epsilon, close to the unperturbed annulus and compatible with the lattice quotient.

proof_stages.label: Complete integrability on the persisted annulus
proof_stages.summary: Because the truncated Hamiltonian is independent of the rescaled fast angle, its restriction to A_epsilon has two independent first integrals, so the four-dimensional annulus carries a completely integrable two-degree-of-freedom Hamiltonian system with Liouville-Arnold coordinates, albeit with epsilon-dependent degeneracy.

proof_stages.label: Adding the final remainder by local straightening
proof_stages.summary: The straightening theorem is applied around A_epsilon, the full Hamiltonian including the ultra-small remainder is written in these coordinates, and localization in a small hyperbolic box plus persistence gives a final normally hyperbolic manifold whose internal dynamics is an arbitrarily small perturbation of the integrable one.

proof_stages.label: KAM tori and compact invariant cylinder
proof_stages.summary: A KAM/Herman theorem, with attention to the vanishing nondegeneracy as epsilon tends to zero, supplies invariant two-tori in the zero-energy intersection, and the region between two such tori is the desired compact invariant cylinder naturally associated with the original annulus.

examples.name: Hypersurface characteristic foliation
examples.note: In response to a question, a coisotropic hypersurface is used to make characteristic foliations concrete: the characteristic leaves are the Hamiltonian flow lines, illustrating why only one derivative is lost.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker applies the characteristic-foliation construction to the hypersurface case, identifying the leaves as Hamiltonian flow lines of a defining Hamiltonian

passes.reason: A hypersurface is the codimension-one/maximal proper coisotropic case with one-dimensional characteristic leaves, used only to illustrate the characteristic foliation.
an ordinary hypersurface/coisotropic characteristic foliation used only to illustrate the general construction
A standard hypersurface characteristic foliation is used illustratively as the codimension-one coisotropic case whose leaves are Hamiltonian flow lines.
A hypersurface is the maximal-dimensional proper coisotropic case, with a one-dimensional characteristic foliation, and it is used only as an illustrative special case.
A hypersurface characteristic foliation is the standard codimension-one instance of the coisotropic characteristic distribution, used illustratively rather than to refute a claim.
A hypersurface characteristic foliation is the standard codimension-one coisotropic case used to illustrate integrability, not an obstruction or sporadic/symmetric/extremal object.
Used only to illustrate characteristic foliations; a hypersurface is the codimension-one/maximal proper coisotropic case.
A hypersurface characteristic foliation is used as a standard illustrative coisotropic case, not to refute a claim.
Used only as the standard illustrative case where characteristic leaves are Hamiltonian flow lines, with no sporadic, symmetric, degenerate, or optimal structure invoked.
A hypersurface is the codimension-one, hence largest proper/minimal-characteristic-rank, coisotropic case used only to illustrate characteristic foliations.
an ordinary hypersurface characteristic foliation is used illustratively to explain coisotropic characteristic leaves, not to refute a claim
Used as a standard illustrative hypersurface/coisotropic case where characteristic leaves are Hamiltonian flow lines, not to refute a claim.

examples.name: Planar fixed-point rescaling analogy
examples.note: The familiar maneuver of rescaling near a planar hyperbolic fixed point is invoked only as an analogy for the hyperbolic-coordinate scaling used in the construction.
examples.engagement_reason: the planar fixed-point rescaling is invoked only as an analogy for the rescaling being done in the main system, without working through a planar fixed-point example

passes.reason: A planar fixed point is a zero-dimensional minimal invariant object used as a simple rescaling analogy, not as an obstruction.
a planar fixed point is the zero-dimensional minimal invariant-set model used only as a rescaling analogy
The planar fixed point is used only as a toy rescaling analogy, and as a zero-dimensional invariant set it is the minimal/base case.
A planar fixed point is the zero-dimensional/minimal invariant-manifold model used as a rescaling analogy, not to disprove any claim.
The planar fixed-point rescaling is a routine local dynamical-systems analogy for the coordinate change, not a sporadic, extremal, or adversarial example.
A planar fixed point is a zero-dimensional minimal invariant-object base case used only as a rescaling analogy.
Used as a heuristic analogy for the rescaling step; a planar fixed point is a minimal, zero-dimensional dynamical base case.
The planar fixed point is invoked only as a simple rescaling analogy, and as an invariant object it is the zero-dimensional minimal case.
Used as a local analogy for rescaling near an equilibrium, and a fixed point is a collapsed zero-dimensional invariant orbit.
The planar fixed point is invoked only as a simple local rescaling analogy, and as a zero-dimensional invariant set it is a minimal base case.
the planar fixed point is invoked only as a rescaling analogy, and a fixed point is the zero-dimensional minimal invariant case
The planar fixed-point rescaling is invoked as an ordinary local analogy for the change of variables, not as a structurally special object or counterexample.
